title: DUCTILE: Agentic LLM Orchestration of Engineering Analysis in Product Development Practice
author: Pradas-Gomez, Alejandro, Brahma, Arindam, Isaksson, Ola
summary: DUCTILE presents an agentic LLM orchestration framework for automating engineering analysis tasks in product development. The system separates adaptive decision-making by an LLM agent from deterministic execution by verified engineering tools, allowing automation to handle input deviations in format, units, naming conventions, and methodology that would break traditional scripted pipelines. Demonstrated on aerospace structural analysis, the approach maintains methodological compliance while adapting to evolving product ecosystems. The paper reveals practical implications: while removing mundane tasks, agentic automation creates new supervisory burdens and reshapes the nature of engineering work itself.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2603.10249 (cs) [Submitted on 10 Mar 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 17 Mar 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: DUCTILE: Agentic LLM Orchestration of Engineering Analysis in Product Development Practice Authors: Alejandro Pradas-Gomez , Arindam Brahma , Ola Isaksson View a PDF of the paper titled DUCTILE: Agentic LLM Orchestration of Engineering Analysis in Product Development Practice, by Alejandro Pradas-Gomez and 2 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Engineering analysis automation in product development relies on rigid interfaces between tools, data formats and documented processes. When these interfaces change, as they routinely do as the product evolves in the engineering ecosystem, the automation support breaks. This paper presents a DUCTILE (Delegated, User-supervised Coordination of Tool- and document-Integrated LLM-Enabled) agentic orchestration, an approach for developing, executing and evaluating LLM-based agentic automation support of engineering analysis tasks. The approach separates adaptive orchestration, performed by the LLM agent, from deterministic execution, performed by verified engineering tools. The agent interprets documented design practices, inspects input data and adapts the processing path, while the engineer supervises and exercises final judgment. DUCTILE is demonstrated on an industrial structural analysis task at an aerospace manufacturer, where the agent handled input deviations in format, units, naming conventions and methodology that would break traditional scripted pipelines. Evaluation against expert-defined acceptance criteria and deployment with practicing engineers confirm that the approach produces correct, methodologically compliant results across 10 repeated independent runs. The paper discusses the paradigm shift and the practical consequences of adopting agentic automation, including unintended effects on the nature of engineering work when removing mundane tasks and creating an exhausting supervisory role. takeaways: Separate LLM-driven orchestration from deterministic tool execution to handle format/unit/naming deviations gracefully
Agent interprets documented design practices and adapts processing paths while engineers retain supervisory judgment
Validated across 10 independent runs on aerospace structural analysis with expert-defined acceptance criteria
Agentic automation trades mundane task removal for exhausting supervisory roles, reshaping engineering workflows

title: https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/23
summary: This article explores emerging agentic design patterns in 2026, examining how AI agents are structured and deployed in production systems. It discusses architectural approaches for building autonomous agents, integration patterns with existing infrastructure, and practical considerations for maintaining control and reliability. The piece provides insights into the evolving landscape of agent-based applications and offers guidance for teams implementing these systems at scale.
takeaways: Agent architectures increasingly emphasize modularity and composability for complex multi-step workflows
Control mechanisms and safety guardrails are critical for production agent systems
Integration with existing tools and APIs remains essential for practical agent deployment
Monitoring and observability patterns differ significantly from traditional application design
Cost optimization and token efficiency drive architectural decisions in LLM-based agents

title: zhongweixie/A-Survey-on-AI-Agent-Harness · Datasets at Hugging Face
summary: This entry appears to be a dataset or collection entry on Hugging Face related to AI agent harness design and executable constraints. The associated paper 'ContextCov' focuses on deriving and enforcing executable constraints from agent instruction files, presenting techniques for improving AI agent reliability through constraint extraction and validation. The work is relevant to researchers developing robust autonomous agents and those interested in formal verification methods for AI system behavior control.
content: ContextCov: Deriving and Enforcing Executable Constraints from Agent Instruction Files Paper • 2603.00822 • Published May 4
takeaways: Derives executable constraints automatically from agent instruction files to improve behavioral compliance
Enforces constraints during agent execution to prevent policy violations and unsafe actions
Addresses challenge of translating natural language agent instructions into formal, verifiable requirements
Relevant for building more reliable and controllable autonomous AI agents

title: Paper page - From Model Scaling to System Scaling: Scaling the Harness in Agentic AI
summary: This article examines the transition from scaling individual AI models to scaling complete agentic AI systems, focusing on the architectural harness that orchestrates agent behavior. The key insight is that system-level scaling requires rethinking infrastructure beyond model capacity—addressing orchestration, resource management, state handling, and inter-agent communication. Technical readers should care because as agentic systems move to production, bottlenecks shift from compute to integration; understanding harness design principles becomes critical for building reliable, scalable multi-agent deployments that can handle real-world complexity and distributed workloads.
content: to read Collection 48 items • Updated 29 minutes ago
takeaways: Model scaling alone insufficient; harness architecture becomes the bottleneck for agentic AI systems.
Orchestration, state management, and communication patterns must be co-designed with agent capabilities.
System-level scaling requires rethinking infrastructure: resource allocation, fault tolerance, and monitoring.
Harness design prioritizes composability and modularity to support heterogeneous agent compositions.
